speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to put on some bars. When I tell you, you just read what it says, OK? Can you do it through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. Couple more seconds. There's a first for everything I guess, huh? [INAUDIBLE] [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today, August 25, 1995, Beth Kleid will be interviewing Betty Daniel in Santa Monica, California, USA. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember to bring the book for my daughter here to put on her couch.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Beth Kleid. It's K-L-E-I-D. It's August 25, 1995, and I have the great pleasure of interviewing Betty Daniel. We're in Santa Monica, California in the United States of America. Betty, what was your name at birth, and can you spell it for us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth is Braindel Helfer, H-E-L-F-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Braindel?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-A-I-N-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My day of birth is August 20, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am just turned, a few days ago, 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Chrzanow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-R-Z-A-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what big city or town was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the city is not far away from the well known Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a family of five, three brothers and a sister. I survived with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's occupation when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, can you talk a little about your family life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have very good recollection. Being the baby of the family, I was treated a little special. And a nice home. I would say my father struggling with to make a living, after he came home from being five years in Siberia from the first World War. And I tried to help by father with thread the needles, cleaning up the garments, delivering-- and occasionally I got a little tip, which was very welcome-- and to rip old garments to make all over again if the material was good. But as a whole, my mother, housewife, religious observant. And good recollections.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You observed Jewish holidays. Can you remember any of those times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the religious and the upbring, I appreciate my mother's effort to send me to a Jewish school by [NON-ENGLISH]. And until today, I can read and write Yiddish, which the language was at that time, and Polish, which I learned in elementary school. Went through, then later on in the years, I wanted to go to a school away from home, [BACKGROUND NOISE] but finances did not allow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you attended the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early teens. I would say 13, 15, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your sister go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went too, yes. Yes, and the brothers helping out the family to sustain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close? Were the children close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly close, yes, fairly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember I used to play volleyball at school, and little activities. I liked to go in to watch ice skating. I was dreaming to go. And I watched [NON-ENGLISH], which I was satisfied. And winter time was the snow, which until today I liked. I like the snow, but I wouldn't like to live in a country where the snow is. I loved the mountains. And this was the general activities. A little in the woods we had the close by to pick up blueberries, and little, I think, mushrooms too. So it was a little, it was a little variety. Nothing can compare to think of today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have cousins nearby, aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had cousins, and my aunts that lived not far. And they used to come. And they used to bring the children to stay for the summer, which we were very fortunate to, to be able to treat them. And the same times, we gained too, because if we went to a farm to have some fresh milk, fresh, fresh cheeses, sour, sour yogurt. So it was real nice. That was a real vacation time. And holidays, were very observant too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers have bar mitzvahs?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I don't remember having them. I don't think whether I even know about this word bar mitzvah. If they went, maybe they had only in the temple. But I don't recall any of the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family went to a temple in your town? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we even had in our close by on our back yard. There was one part of the house, of the next door house, that it was like small steeple. Like they call it like a little house where the congregants got together and they were praying. But the main synagogue was we had to walk. And everything was close by, was in a walking distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- what was the Jewish community like in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community was very nice, especially Shabbat. I remember I used to go Saturday with my mother to visit someone. And yes, I remember too that when it was, I think, Purim, [NON-ENGLISH], the offering, used to go with a little plate to put on a cube of chocolate on a piece of cake, and an apple if we could afford. But I remember very well the cube of chocolate. Not a block of chocolate, how you call it, but a cube, a small piece of chocolate. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That was candies. No, candies I don't remember, but I remember very vividly the chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams for yourself when you were a, a girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I really don't remember exactly. Being still in a young age, I wanted-- I didn't know the existence-- oh, yes, I, I remember I went visiting to my aunt, so I saw another town, which opened my eyes a little. But there was no time to dream. No, no, I was too young, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work for your family business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the last-- just before the war, I think, there was the family, we got the little business, where to iron the clothes. They call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was like a heavy box and a wax inside. It used to be operated by hand. And later on, it was operated by a motor, so the electric one was much easier. And I to put, like roll up the, the [BACKGROUND NOISE] clothes, sheets, and shirts, and towels, and make it like a roll, and to roll it under this, which operated the [NON-ENGLISH]. And it came out with a shine, just like ironing. You didn't have to iron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the whole family work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was only one who operated it. It was physical work. And sometime my sister helped me too. My mother occasionally too, yes, but she was practically the housewife. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And my father was busy. The brothers did not help, no. It was like a source of the income going into the war. There was people come in and exchange food for labor. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I did some too, some work, fixing nylons. Not nylons, but silk stockings. When they have the runs, there is a special needle to pick it up and to, not to sew them away, but to fix them. And that was a very tedious work, and I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, can you think of any incidents of anti-Semitism that you faced while growing up in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitism, especially, personally myself, I don't know. But I know that we didn't go close-- oh yes, in school I remember when we had to, we had to stand up when the Christians said their prayers. We had our hands together, and we stood up. But I don't remember exactly whether I was-- in my years, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about other members of your family? Did you hear them tell stories about any type of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to, I used to hear the word Jew, especially on Passover, that when they say that we used to put in the blood into the matzos. I-- no. No, I wouldn't say. As a matter of fact, I had this a friend that was not Jewish, a personal friend. And I think after the war, I, I contacted her. She sent me a picture, her picture, to Paris even, yeah. But recently, I didn't hear from her, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, when did things start to change? When did you notice a change taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the, [SIGHS] when the war broke out in 1939, in September or August, yeah, we were-- we knew that there was the persecution of Jews. And we had to go to work for the German. Nothing too serious yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I personally was not alert or aware of it yet, until later on when it was-- when they caught my brother to-- well, it was at night, knocking at the door, coming in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe that incident, describe what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to, they call it [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means that they knock at the door and come in, and we all, of course, afraid. Sometimes they took someone, sometimes they didn't. It depends who's luck was it. So my brother, my oldest brother was the first one to go to the camps, to the working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he? And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he must have been that time maybe in the late-- just a minute. '33-- he must have been in the early 20s that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Herschel.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the oldest brother. He went and we knew that he's in a working camp, and nothing much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before the knock came on the door, was there talk? Did you know that this was going to happen? Were your parents worried?

speaker: interviewee
text: We expected something. But there was talking between us, and exchanging news. And my father was very, very, delicate. He says, oh-- he was very sorry that he lived through a time that we have to be fearful and not to be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after your brother Herschel was taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my brother Herschel was taken away, I think there was the middle brother, [? Hidel. ?] He was taken away too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he was too, maybe a few years different. Maybe a year or two different the brothers were. So he was taken, I think, from the working from the shop where we use to work for the, for the military. So over there, they used to take and pick up some workers too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from him, we didn't have any sign. And I didn't know until today were any sign. But from the older brother, I know that there was an accident where he used to work, and he died. He got killed, it's an accident. He fell down. And he was buried at the camp over there. So how was it in your home after the two brothers left? What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, myself, and my sister, and my father, we used to-- we went to the shop to work for the German. Sometimes it was day time, sometimes it was night, night shifts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the uniforms. I used to though-- I used to do hand work. My sister, I think, and my father did operating in a machine. And I used to work, hand work. I remember I used to join the collar. It was, it was like finishing from the garment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about amongst yourselves with the people who you worked with, the other Jewish people, and your family? What did-- did you have an idea of what might be happening to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I personally have very little recollection about the-- as I got very little is that how to survive. And the war is we try to hear something about the war. Somebody maybe heard something from a radio, from hiding, about the war, at the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, at that time, think you would-- did you know what was happening with your brothers? Did you hear word about what they were doing, the two brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I remember I had one teacher in elementary school, and he was not Jewish. And he was taken to Dachau. And I think about a few months later, or maybe a year later, he, he came back. But he came back very thin, very not to recognize him. So we sort of like got the message that it's not, it's not a picnic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, what your third brother? What had happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My third brother died of TB in 1933, just quite before the war, which was a very big tragedy. My mother was very much mourning after this brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a young boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was the younger one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He must have been in his early-- in his late teens, maybe. Yes, was young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So getting back to the teacher who returned from Dachau, you realized that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But he was-- I think he was taking, now as I gather, he must have taking as a political prisoner, not as a Jew. That must have been. But nothing else. No other recollection I have. But this remained in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came 1943. There was the general cleaning up from Jews in our hometown. They came. They knocked at the door, and everybody to go out. And one of the SS men said, take the keys, because you're going to come back. So myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: About what time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was exactly February 17 or 18, 1943. So my sister, and myself, and parents, we went to the main, main place in the town, like sort of gathered everyone, all the Jews. And there they separated my parents and myself and my sister. My sister and myself, they sent us to a working camp, to Neusalz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, what-- do you remember the last words you exchanged with your parents before you were separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I remember the keys, that they said, take the keys, you're going to go home. So whether they said for me or my sister to take the keys, or the parents. And that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your family brought any things with you when you went out to the square?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't have anything. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- when your parents left and you were separated, did you know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Now as I recollect, I don't, I don't remember exactly. It was sort of like a mixed emotions. It was excitement. Knowing-- we knew about Auschwitz. We didn't know exactly, or maybe I didn't know exactly what's going on over there. It was 1943. From '39 to '43, it was already four years, not quite four years. So it's-- no, I personally don't remember exactly what. But later on, we didn't hear anything from 'em. And we got some messages what's going on in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So getting back to when you were separated, you and your sister were left there. And what happened to you two next, after your parents had separated from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left the parents?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my sister. So that was-- I had here and she had me. So we both together stick together, and we were able to be together all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during the day of the separation, were other older people sent with your parents that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that day a little more?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was us. It was us. We went only to younger generation. The older one were separated. And we were sent to, to another place, like a gathering. And from there, they sent away in groups to places where they needed workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your sister, that same day, were sent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how, how were you-- what was the method of transportation to your next--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were sent, we were sent in trains. I think in trains, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that train ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember, no. I just remember that we were sent to, to a camp, and that was Neusalz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can't remember what the journey was like? It was February, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe February, maybe beginning of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with others from your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, other ones that came from our home town, and maybe from other towns too that they gathered together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what you might have been thinking about during that ride?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] No, no. I cannot remember, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about anything you talked about with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We just looked for the next day and that says it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall about how long that train journey was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that train was not as bad, because we said we were sent to a camp to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were lucky that we didn't go into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you finally got to the destination and the doors opened, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Neusalz. It was with some our home town friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Neusalz is in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Neusalz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Germany. It was a mill, [NON-ENGLISH], I think a cotton mill. [NON-ENGLISH]. The name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your first day there like? What did you see when you got out of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got acquainted. We went to work. And we looked for the day to get our meals. We were hungry. And I have very little recollection from the camps. A matter of fact, I have some sort survivors here that were together with us. And they ask me, Betty, don't you remember that, and that we were in the same room, and I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's try and go back to Neusalz and see if you can describe and remember. What were the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those were livable conditions. Yes, Neusalz was livable conditions. Yes, a matter of fact, I personally have got recollections from Neusalz, because I had a very good kind of work, affordable work, where I could meet some prisoner-- French prisoners of war. And by that type of work, this prisoner could put in some Red Cross goodies into my basket, what I had. And I could share. And I remember now, and until today, I have a habit to part. When I am with my sister, or with anyone, I share, I cut in half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --remember the name of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the French the French prisoner of war, no. No. But there was over there one German. And I'm sure that he has seen it and he ignored it. So that was a help. So it was-- I was lucky. And then I had a woman too, who, every day, she put in one place like a container with soup for me. And one German woman gave me a toothbrush, which I could bring it in the camp and exchange for some soup. If I had another toothbrush, I would. So as my recollection, as I remember Neusalz, the good things, that I could survive over there, with my sister. Though my sister didn't have this kind of work. She had no opportunities to help herself. So both together, we shared and we survived Neusalz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, the German women who you just mentioned, she worked in the factory with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she worked next to me. And I supplied her with, with the able, that she would be able to work the waltzes. That it goes like a wooden waltz that I brought from the French prisoner to supply her. So it was a good connection. I was lucky with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- did you feel scared that someone might find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, this women, if she would, if she would be caught, she would be maybe punished. But she tried to help us. She was very compassionate. I wish if I would see her now, if I would know her name. And I am grateful to her, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty, what was your daily routine like in Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The daily routine was getting up and to count us, [INAUDIBLE] on the place, and then to march to the work, because the factory was close by. The barracks were close by to the factory. And to go to rush to be on time, not to be late, and to come back. Once I worked the night shift, which until today, I remember very, very well. It's the worst time is our 1, 2, and 3 o'clock in the morning. That was the worst hour. You fell asleep. And daytime, we could visit a little, and clean up the barracks to keep it clean, to do some house chores, because there were inspections in the barracks, duties, certain duties. And if one could have a duty in the kitchen, that was a reward. You could have another slice of bread, or another potato, or anything. It was-- so my recollection about Neusalz was not bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you're sleeping conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had bunk beds, up and down. And I had my sister. I think we slept with my sister together, yes. So we kept warm at night--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: --cuddling.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the other prisoners there? What did you talk about with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall very little at the barracks. I remember-- well, I remember the work, and that helped me to survive, and to be helpful to my sister too. And hoping that one day we will, we will get together and, and have our parents. But later on, it went too far. It was, it was a little less and less hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you feel a sense of hope during your time at Neusalz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope? Yes. We never lost hope. One day, I remember there was one French soldier who was a little, little guy. And he was Jewish. And he told us once when Rosh Hashanah comes up. He was really influential, and he knew. So he tried to help. So it was really very, very helpful, and optimistic too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK?
